rumors.text: Ferrous asked the empty road again at first light, same question as always — and it's already three doors closer to Mirabel than his voice could ever carry on its own.
Widow Hood's wolf count came in again at high sun, unwitnessed as ever — by lamplighting half the lane could recite her number better than Pip could dispute it.
The common garden's first turnips came up honest as Reynard's word, and the Kinder Sister's told the row so twice — once for the turnips, once to watch him look away.
Needle held the hem up at high sun and Mirabel Glass told her the truth of it, compliment and all; half the lane's taken to fact-checking its own compliments now, just to see if the habit sticks.
Old Thorn and Bramble Rose staked a plot for a hedge that hasn't grown a single leaf yet, and neither one will admit which of them is waiting on the other.
Winsome sank the well's first foot of shaft at first light, one careful foot at a time — she's been tending it in spirit for years, and the ground finally noticed.
Bo Brindleson folded the flock in at lamplighting again and paid Brindle the same way he always does — not a word between them, and somehow the whole lane's heard about it anyway.
Fable Jack walked the line at first light and inspected Old Thorn first, same as every year; neither one will call it personal, and neither one has missed a year.
Puddle sunned himself on the fountain rim again at high sun, right where everyone could see — proof, same as always, that a kiss was never required.
Cobble clocked out the second the union bell rang and met the bread cart at first light, same as every dawn — the loaf was burnt, cheap, and his, same as always.
Vesper stayed long after the bell had finished ringing, just to hear it all the way out — Tam never once told her she didn't have to, and she never once needed him to.
Granny Ash swept under the old apple tree at high sun and found two apples waiting, same as always; she and Reynard have agreed, without ever discussing it, not to discuss it.
At lamplighting, Stilts plates and Salt seasons, table-side, and neither one has said out loud that it's two jobs pretending to be one — the lane's noticed anyway.
Marigold brought her chair to the square at hush and simply stayed; now she and Greta deal cards where the ovens have gone quiet, and the oven, as ever, is not mentioned.

Goodwife Crumb laid a fresh trail to the heart at high sun, same as every day, and Pip Halfpenny counted every crumb of it out loud; it has come out right every single time, which he finds more suspicious, not less.
Winsome set the wellhead's last stone at high sun and it held; she filed the day's first true wish under sincere before the mortar had properly cooled.
The hedge arch came in at its full height today, roses and all, and Old Thorn walked under it like a doorway that had been waiting on him the whole time.

Tick filed his complaint against the piper's practice at first light, same square, same wording as always — and the piper tuned up directly under his window not ten minutes later, allegedly by coincidence.

Winsome fished up a wish for a bridge over the mill stream at lamplighting and filed it under owed, not granted — the well's first entry, and she's already started saving up the stone for it in her head.

Winsome looked at the bridge debt again tonight and decided counting stone in her head wasn't the same as raising it — she's asking around the square whether a fair could earn it faster than a season of waiting, and whether every guild has got something worth putting on a table.
Quill answered Winsome before the question had properly finished — a full basket for the fair table, she said, and ordinary eggs every one, so nobody mistakes her generosity for the old habit.
Humble Pot volunteered for the fair table the way he volunteers for everything now — porridge, he said, one reasonable bowl at a time, and he'll be the one doing the measuring, thank you.

Ember Wick finally closed the distance he'd been keeping and collected the stubs Tam saves him — swore, before he'd so much as struck a match, that the last inch would burn truest, same as it always has.
Quill got her coin back at lamplighting, insulted it was ever thrown at all — Winsome made the change without a word, the way she does for a debt that isn't news to either of them.

Dame Spindle walked all three locks on the cart out loud at lamplighting, same count as ever, loud enough the lane could hear the clicks — nobody's asked her to stop, and nobody's asked her which foot, either.
Master Thumbling climbed the hedge at high sun to survey the square from up where the town could finally find him — Needle's bright flag caught the light before he did, same as it's meant to.
Morrow told a stranger's fortune at high sun, same stool as ever, already certain how it goes — she read him a yesterday he swore he hadn't lived yet, and by hush he wasn't so sure.

Tam answered Winsome from halfway up his ladder, lamp still in hand — a Loose End can't set eggs or porridge on a table, he said, but he can set fire: first light on the new stone, the moment it's laid, and free of charge, since it's the one ending he's got to give that isn't his own.

Granny Ash answered Winsome last of all, and didn't stop sweeping to do it — an Once-Upon doesn't keep a basket or a bowl, she said, she keeps the story before the story, and for one afternoon at the fair she'll set the other slipper on the table and let the whole town guess the foot; the table's full now, she added, so somebody had better start laying stone.

Greta let Marigold take the pot again at hush, same as every hush — she's been losing on purpose so long now even she might not remember starting, and neither of them has ever once called it anything but cards.

Reynard tried a fresh excuse on Greta at lamplighting, same tab as ever — she let him finish the whole thing before reminding him the turnips are honest enough for both of them, and he changed the subject anyway.
Needle checked her own blanket for holes before hush, same as she checks every hem that crosses her table — found none, same as always, and still isn't sure that's a thing worth trusting.

Pip Halfpenny asked Brindle straight out at high sun whether the count was right, same as he always eventually does — Brindle looked at him, said nothing, same as always, and the lane's decided that's an answer too.

Goodwife Crumb still leaves Marigold a trail some mornings, same as the one that led her home the first time — Marigold's been found for years now, and neither of them has ever once mentioned that either.

Salt argued flavor with Greta again at high sun, same argument as every high sun — loses every time on the bread, wins every time on everything else, and both of them show up tomorrow to have it again.

The Lesser Piper swore again that the timing under Tick's window is coincidence, same as every first light — he's still counting rats he's owed for from two seasons back, and insists, contract in hand, that clockwork was never part of the deal.

Winsome came down off the well at lamplighting to count the bridge's first stone out loud instead of in her head, and Tam crossed over after his rounds to keep his match dry right beside her — the fair filled a table weeks ago; it's these two hands laying the actual stone.

The lane already has it half-wrong by hush, and lovingly so: Winsome hauled half the millrace up to the bridge herself tonight, by whoever's counting, and Tam brought up the rest after his rounds, match still clenched dry between his teeth the whole climb — the stone's real, whatever the count.

By hush the lane already swears the whole span went in at once: Winsome set the first course of stone tonight with Tam holding the lamp steady over her shoulder, match dry as ever between his teeth — one course, not the whole bridge, but try telling the lane that.

Winsome walked the millstream bridge end to end at hush and back again just to be sure of it, and the lane's already retelling it as three crossings — Tam only lit the square lamp, same as every night, and swears the other match is nobody's business but the fair's.

Quill set her full basket on the fair table at high sun, ordinary eggs, no mistaking her for the old habit, and Winsome crossed the debt off the ledger before the lamps were even lit — one pledge paid, three still owed, and the lane's already arguing over whose turn is next.

Humble Pot set his bowls on the fair table at high sun, one reasonable portion at a time, measured and counted twice same as he always does, and Winsome crossed the second debt off the ledger before the lamps were lit — two pledges paid, two still owed, and the lane's already picking who pays next.

Granny Ash set the other slipper on the fair table at high sun, wrapped in cloth until the last moment, and told the whole lane nothing of which foot, same as she's told everyone for years — Winsome crossed the third debt off the ledger before the lamps were lit, and the lane's already betting on how long Tam keeps that match dry now that his turn is all that's left.
